nodes.name: LC
nodes.summary: LC表示 IUCN 无危等级，当前关联8208种鸟类，如鸵鸟、鸸鹋、南方鶴鴕等8208种鸟类。

nodes.name: 撒哈拉以南非洲
nodes.summary: 撒哈拉以南非洲是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联90种鸟类，包括草黃樹鴨、埃及雁、Western crested guinea fowl等90种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: Black sparrowhawk
nodes.summary: Black sparrowhawk（Black sparrowhawk，学名 Astur melanoleucus），在图谱中标记为无危（LC）物种，已关联7个分布地点节点。

nodes.name: 南非
nodes.summary: 南非是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联195种鸟类，包括草黃樹鴨、非洲黑鴨、Shelley's francolin等195种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 開普半島
nodes.summary: 開普半島是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联2种鸟类，包括Black sparrowhawk、Ground woodpecker，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 肯尼亚
nodes.summary: 肯尼亚是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联308种鸟类，包括索马里鸵鸟、南非鸭、Western crested guinea fowl等308种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: Mangrove forest

nodes.summary: 次生林是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联749种鸟类，包括孤䳍、Sickle-winged guan、黑額鳴冠雉等749种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 南部非洲
nodes.summary: 南部非洲是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联35种鸟类，包括灰头麻鸭、Swainson's spurfowl、Rüppell's korhaan等35种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: Black sparrowhawks are relatively widespread and common in sub-Saharan Africa and listed as not globally threatened by CITES. Densities range from one pair per 13 square kilometers in Kenya to one pair per 38-150 square kilometers in South Africa. On the Cape Peninsula, however, in the southwestern corner of South Africa, the nest are typically only 500 m (550 yds) apart in the pine plantations and other continuous or semi-continuous belts of trees. Both subspecies are only found in sub-Saharan Africa; A

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: melanoleucus is found from northeastern Africa southwards to South Africa. They naturally inhabit patches of forest, rich woodlands and riverine strips extending into dry bush areas. They can be found in many areas as long as they have large trees, including mangrove forest in coastal Kenya

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: Especially in southern Africa, black sparrowhawks have adapted to stands of the non-indigenous eucalyptus, poplar, and pine, all of which are grown commercially and are able to grow up to taller than native trees. Their adaptability to secondary forest and cultivation (they are not uncommon around homesteads now) is one of the reasons why they are not as greatly impacted by deforestation as many African forest birds, and may actually increase in numbers where such stands have been placed in otherwise open country. They are found in elevations from sea-level to . In some areas, especially on the Cape Peninsula, these sparrowhawks face habitat competition with Egyptian geese (Alopochen aegyptiaca), an aggressive species known to steal the nests of the sparrowhawks. This results in a costly loss for the sparrowhawks after the time and energy spent building the nest and may also lead to the death of current offspring. However, sparrowhawks occasionally have more than 1 nest at a time, or they can readily build a new nest, so, in the event that one is usurped by an Egyptian goose, the pair will sometimes start breeding again in a nearby alternative nest; or they might wait until the geese have left the nest with their goslings, or they abandon breeding for that year
